coredata.dc:title: Up-conversion fluorescent carbon quantum dots decorated covalent triazine frameworks as efficient metal-free photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yueling
Huang, Guocheng
Gao, Yanxin
Chen, Qiaoshan
Bi, Jinhong
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic hydrogen production holds great promise for alleviating the energy shortage through effective photo-to-chemical conversion, and the development of visible-light responsive, low-cost and sustainable photocatalysts remains key priority. In this study, carbon quantum dots/covalent triazine-based framework (CQDs/CTF) non-metallic photocatalyst was constructed through a simple impregnation method for photocatalytic H2 evolution. Upon 0.24% CQDs loading, a three-fold enhanced H2 production activity of 102 μmol∙g−1∙h−1 was achieved compared with pristine CTF-1 (34.5 μmol∙g−1∙h−1). Photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical study revealed carbon quantum dots served as the electron libraries, which was conducive to facilitate electron capture and promote the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in CTF-1. Notably, the excitation-independent up-conversion fluorescent characteristics of CQDs endowed the catalysts broadened visible-light response range and higher solar energy utilization efficiency. This study deepens insights into the mechanism of CQDs modification and paves a trustworthy strategy for harvesting visible-light-driven metal-free photocatalyst with highly-active and robust performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Covalent triazine-based frameworks
Visible-light photocatalysis
Solar energy conversion
Water splitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Cx/CTF.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF samples.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s for CTF-1 and C0.24/CTF composite.
(a) SEM image of pristine CTF-1; (b) TEM and HRTEM (insert) images of C0.24/CTF composite; (c) HRTEM image of pristine CQDs; (d) Particle size distribution of CQDs in C0.24/CTF composite.

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF; Photoluminescence spectra of CQDs at excited wavelengths of (b) 300–500 nm and (c) 680–780 nm; (d) Photoluminescence spectra of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF composites.
(a) photocurrent responses and (b) Nyquist plots of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF composites.

(a) H2 evolution performance of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF; (b) Stability and (c) wavelength dependence of H2 evolution rate for C0.24/CTF; (d) Photocurrent response of CTF-1 and C0.24/CTF samples under visible light (λ = 500 nm).
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic H2 evolution process on metal-free Cx/CTF photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Up-conversion fluorescent carbon quantum dots decorated covalent triazine frameworks as efficient metal-free photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian 350108, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic hydrogen production holds great promise for alleviating the energy shortage through effective photo-to-chemical conversion, and the development of visible-light responsive, low-cost and sustainable photocatalysts remains key priority. In this study, carbon quantum dots/covalent triazine-based framework (CQDs/CTF) non-metallic photocatalyst was constructed through a simple impregnation method for photocatalytic H2 evolution. Upon 0.24% CQDs loading, a three-fold enhanced H2 production activity of 102 μmol∙g−1∙h−1 was achieved compared with pristine CTF-1 (34.5 μmol∙g−1∙h−1). Photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical study revealed carbon quantum dots served as the electron libraries, which was conducive to facilitate electron capture and promote the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in CTF-1. Notably, the excitation-independent up-conversion fluorescent characteristics of CQDs endowed the catalysts broadened visible-light response range and higher solar energy utilization efficiency. This study deepens insights into the mechanism of CQDs modification and paves a trustworthy strategy for harvesting visible-light-driven metal-free photocatalyst with highly-active and robust performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The massive consumption of fossil energy brought about increasingly severe issues of environmental deterioration and energy crisis. In this scenario, searching renewable energy source is in urgent need, exemplified by the technology of photocatalysis which could convert inexhaustible solar energy to clean hydrogen energy through water splitting [1–4]. During the past decades, a series of semiconductor materials including metal oxides, metal sulfides, metal-organic frameworks and versatile modifying strategies like doping, constructing heterojunction were developed for photocatalytic H2 evolution [5–9]. However, most of the reported photocatalysts generally carry defects, such as inefficient absorption of visible light (e.g. Nb2O5, Fe2O3, Zn0.8Cd0.2S etc) [10–12] and weak stability (e.g. Cu2O, CdS/ZnO, Sm2Ti2S2O5 etc) [13–15]. Additionally, the metal ions released through catalyst photocorrosion could cause detrimental impact on the environment and human body. In terms of economic and environmental benefits, exploring metal-free catalysts is therefore a desirable strategy to reduce cost as well as avoid residual metal ions.

As a typical metal-free semiconductor, covalent triazine-based frameworks (CTFs) is widely being used in fields of environmental purification and energy conversion recently owing to its suitable energy band structure, facile fabrication and excellent stability [16,17]. Regrettably, due to the limited visible-light response range and rapid photoinduced carriers recombination, the photocatalytic performance of the original CTF is yet unsatisfactory [18]. To improve the performance of CTF, methods like optimizing synthesis strategies [19], establishing unique crystal structures [20] and loading precious metal catalysts [21] have been adopted. Specifically, Yadav et al. developed a two-dimensional CTFs from the precursors of cyanuric chloride and animide, and applied it to photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Their results showed that the yield of formic acid (HCOOH) reached as high of 204.14 μmol within 2 h [22]. Another example comes from Meier et al. who synthesized CTF-1 by a liquid phase polymerization method and achieved a photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 35 μmol∙g−1∙h−1 [23]. Nevertheless, the bottlenecks like severe recombination of photogenerated charges and inadequate active sites in CTFs still remained unsolved, leaving great demand for exploiting more suitable modification strategy of CTFs.

Currently, quantum dots (QDs) that features in ultra-small size, quantum confinement effect, high photochemical stability and environmental friendliness has won the favor of researchers and been widely applied in the fields of photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, sensing and bioimaging [24–27]. Compared to precious metal promoters, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were endowed with advantages of low cost, large storage capacity and good electrical conductivity. Intriguingly, CQDs possesses excellent photoelectron transmission characteristics and could capture long-wavelength light to emit short-wavelength light through the up-conversion effect originated from the different emission traps on surface [28]. So far, various photocatalysts modified with CQDs, such as TiO2, oxygen-rich TiO2 and WO3 have been reported and applied for degradation of tetracycline, RhB and cyclohexane under simulated sunlight [29–31]. The CQDs embedded in these composite materials play a vital role in boosting the photocatalytic activity via comprehensive mechanisms including broadening absorption spectrum of sunlight and facilitating electron transport/storage due to the anti-stokes effect and the conjugated π structure, respectively [32]. Inspired by the abovementioned advantages, CQDs are expected to serve as ideal alternative for precious metals to act as environmental-friendly non-metallic accelerator.

To verify the hypothesis, the typical covalent triazine framework CTF-1 was modified with CQDs through a facile impregnation method for efficient H2 production under stimulated sunlight irradiation. The structure, morphology and photoelectric property of the decorated CTF-1 were systematically characterized. Moreover, the underlying mechanisms were clarified with the aid of a series of advanced measurements like photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical analysis. This work paves a novel reference for developing quantum dots modified metal-free catalytic materials.

CTF-1 was prepared through the reported low-temperature polycondensation strategy [33]. In brief, 40 mL of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid and 5.12 g of terephthalonitrile were mixed in a round bottom flask under an ice bath. After solidification, the sample was placed under static condition at room temperature for three days and the product was then washed with dichloromethane and ammonium solution, filtered and redispersed in ammonia solution for 12 h. Subsequently, the sample was centrifuged with excess distilled water and methanol and then subjected to 12 h Soxhlet extraction with methanol at 90 °C and dichloromethane at 70 °C, respectively. Eventually, the obtained solid was placed in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h, and pale-yellow powder was collected.

For the synthesis of CQDs, Na2EDTA was firstly calcined in a tube furnace at 400 °C (ramp rate: 5 °C/min) for 2 h in N2 atmosphere [34]. The dark products were then purified by dispersing in acetone followed by centrifugation. The homogeneous supernatant containing CQDs was collected and then dripped into the CTF-1 suspension that containing 0.2 g CTF-1. The resulted mixture was heated under stirring at 90 °C to remove the solvent and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The obtained complexes with different CQDs content were marked as Cx/CTF (x = 0.08, 0.24, 0.4, 0.8%).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku MiniFlex 600, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded in an IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific) by using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained on the PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, which uses monochromatic Al Kα as the X-ray source. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of samples were obtained using Nova Nano SEM 230 microscope (FEI Corp.) and JEOL JEM 2010 EX instrument, respectively. The average particle size was measured by Nano measurer based on the TEM images. The Brunauer-Emmett Teller (BET) surface area and pore structure of the sample was analyzed on ASAP2020 (Micromeritics Instrument, USA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the tested samples were recorded on a Varian Cary 5000 spectrometer. FL/FS 900 fluorescence spectrometer was used to perform photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy of the photocatalyst.

The CHI650E electrochemical workstation was used to implement the electrochemical test in the conventional three-electrode battery. The platinum electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode were applied as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. A Na2SO4 solution (0.2 M) and a 300 W xenon lamp (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 400 nm filter were utilized as the electrolyte and the test light source, respectively. The process of preparing the working electrode was as follows: the powder photocatalyst (5 mg) was fully immersed in N,N-dimethylformamide (0.5 mL) by ultrasound for 4 h, and then the suspension (10 μL) was dripped on a fluoride-tin oxide glass in size of 0.25 cm2. In order to maintain insulation, the other areas of the glass substrate were coated with epoxy resin, then the electrodes were dried overnight at room temperature. In order to reduce the influence of semiconductor layer thickness, the photocurrent test was performed in the form of backlight. ZAHNER IM6 was utilized to measure the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the mixed solution of K3[Fe(CN)6] (10 mM), K4[Fe(CN)6] (5 mM) and KCl (0.1 M), while the Mott-Schottky plot was measured in Na2SO4 (0.2 M) solution. Under the same condition, the measurement was repeated for three times.

A 100 mL Pyrex glass reaction vessel equipped with a gas circulation system was used for the photocatalytic hydrogen escape activity test. In a specific reaction, photocatalyst powder (20 mg) was added to the ultrapure water (50 mL) which contained triethanolamine (5 mL). By dissolving a certain amount of H2PtCl6, about 1 wt% of Pt could deposit on the catalysts. The entire reaction system was completely free of air before irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm) and the temperature was kept at constant by applying the cooling water. The amount of evolved H2 was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-8A) equipped with a TCD detector. The dependence of H2 evolution on the incident light wavelength was evaluated by changing the incident light through different band-pass filters. The stability experiment of C0.24/CTF was carried out through 5 cycles of photocatalysis experiment (each lasting for 4 h). The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of the photocatalysts were calculated by the following formula: AQY (%) = (2 × H/I) × 100, where H and I were the number of H2 and induced protons, respectively. The induced protons were measured with a calibrated Si photodiode by assuming that the photocatalysts can fully absorb the protons.

The typical covalent triazine-based framework CTF-1 was prepared through low-temperature polycondensation of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid [35] and the carbon quantum dots/covalent triazine-based framework (Cx/CTF) series catalysts were synthesized by simply dripping CQDs suspension into CTF-1, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The crystal structures of the as-synthesized CTF-1 and Cx/CTF were firstly investigated by XRD and FTIR. CTF-1 showed three obvious diffraction peaks at 17.9, 23.7 and 26.9° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2 a), where the peak at 2θ values of 26.9° belonged to its layered stacking structure [16]. Similarly, the synthesized Cx/CTF composites also exhibited three diffraction peaks, indicating the adding of CQDs didn't destruct the layered structure of CTF-1. The significant characteristic peaks of CQDs were not noticed in the synthetics due to the low content and crystallinity of the loaded CQDs [36]. The FTIR spectra of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF composites, as described in Fig. 2b, displayed analogical characteristic curves. The two infrared absorption peaks at wavenumber of 1508 and 1357 cm−1, corresponding to the “C–N″ single bond in the triazine ring [21]. This finding was in accord with the XRD results, demonstrating the main triazine framework of CTF-1 was well maintained after modified by CQDs.

The chemical states of the typical samples CTF-1 and C0.24/CTF were then analyzed by XPS. In Fig. 3 a, it was observed that the C 1s spectrum of CTF-1was composed by four peaks located at 284.6 (graphite-like sp2 C–C bond), 286.7 (cyano carbon CN), 288.0 (sp2 hybrid carbon in the nitrogen-containing aromatic ring N–CN) and 291.3 eV (the characteristic aromatic carbon π−π∗) [37–39]. The appearance of cyano carbon was probably induced by the partial decomposition of the conjugated aromatic systems during the synthesis process [40]. Notably, an additional photoelectron peak at 285.4 eV corresponding to the C–O bond in CQDs was observed in the C0.24/CTF sample, illustrating that CQDs was successfully loaded onto CTF-1 [41]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), CTF-1 and C0.24/CTF both showed a photoelectron peak at 399.2 eV, which was attributed to the pyridine type CN–C in the triazine ring [1].

The typical layered structure of pristine CTF-1 with smooth surface was clearly presented in the SEM and TEM images (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S1) and the CTF-1 lamella could provide sufficient space for the CQDs modification. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the noticeable dark spots indicated that CQDs were well deposited on the surface of CTF-1 and the distribution was quite uniform, which was partially indicated by the white circles. In the selected area HRTEM, the nature of these dark spots became much clear, where the CQDs exhibited the lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane [42]. For comparison, the HRTEM pattern of pristine CQDs were presented in Fig. 4c, which also showed the d-spacings of 0.21 nm. However, partial aggregation was observed in the pristine CQDs sample, further manifesting that favorable dispersity of the CQDs could be achieved when decorated CQDs on the CTF-1 surface. The particle size distribution of CQDs in range of 1.2–2.3 nm was showed in Fig. 4d and the average particle size was measure to be 1.72 nm. The tight contact between CQDs and CTF-1 was favorable for the segmentation of the photoinduced carriers and thus improving the photocatalytic capability [29]. The CTF-1 and Cx/CTF composites all possessed the mesoporous structure, confirming by their type IV nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms containing the H3 hysteresis loop (Fig. S2) [43,44]. From the specific BET parameters summarized in Table S1, it was not surprising that the loading of CQDs had little effect on the porous property of CTF-1 and no obvious change on the surface area was observed after CQDs modification.

The optical property and light-collecting ability of CTF-1 and Cx/CTF series samples were evaluated through UV–vis DRS. The pristine CTF-1 exhibited favorable visible-light absorption capacity and its band gap energy was determined to be 2.94 eV according to the intercept of tangent in the plot of (αhυ)2 versus photon energy (hυ) according to Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. S3a) [45–47]. Based upon the Mott-Schottky analysis, the conduction band (CB) potential of CTF-1 was measured to be −1.03 V vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) and the accordingly valence band (VB) potential was calculated to be +1.91 V vs. NHE (Fig. S3b). After the introduction of CQDs, no obvious shift of light absorption band edge was observed in Cx/CTF series samples (Fig. 5 a), evidencing the loaded CQDs didn't change the energy band structure of CTF-1. Nevertheless, visible-light trapping capacity of CTF-1 was greatly promoted through CQDs modification, probably attributing to the inherent fluorescence property. To demonstrate the speculation, the down-conversion and up-conversion PL behavior of CQDs was studied by applying different excited light wavelengths from 300 to 780 nm. Under short wavelength range, the strongest emission peak of CQDs moved towards longer wavelength from 410 to 520 nm with reduced intensity as the excited light altered from 300 to 500 nm, exhibiting the excitation-dependent down-conversion behavior (Fig. 5b). This phenomenon was widely detected in the luminescent carbon materials due to optical selection of the surface defect states near the CQDs Fermi level [48,49]. Significantly, excitation-independent up-conversion behavior of CQDs was observed in long wavelength range and the maximum peaks of PL spectra all located around 460 nm under different excitation lights from 680 to 780 nm (Fig. 5c). The up-converted emission characteristic of CQDs was resulted from the anti-Stokes type emission, capturing long-wavelength visible light and converting to short-wavelength light [50–52]. The emissive shorter-wavelength visible light could be utilized for stimulating CTF-1. It is reasonable that the modified CQDs could serve as light collecting component to improve the visible-light utilization of CTF-1, which is conductive to enhancing its photocatalytic performance.

To explore separation efficiency of the photoinduced carriers on CQDs loaded samples, electrochemical analysis was performed to characterize the charge transport process. The CTF-1 and Cx/CTF composites all exhibited distinguished photoelectrochemical stability, illustrated by the stable photocurrent responses after visible-light irradiation for several on-off cycles (Fig. 6 a). Notably, the photocurrent intensities of Cx/CTF were enhanced through iron modification and the highest intensity was achieved in C0.24/CTF, indicating CQDs doping was conducive to accelerating charge separation and mobility. To gain deeper insight of the charge transfer process, EIS analysis was conducted and the Nyquist diagram was presented in Fig. 6b. Smaller semicircle sizes of EIS curves were observed in Cx/CTF composites than that of CTF-1, elucidating the lower charge transfer resistances [53–55]. The EIS results were consistent with the photocurrent response trend and the highest electron transfer efficiency was obtained in C0.24/CTF. Furthermore, the modification of CQDs effectively suppressed the recombination of photoinduced electrons and hole, verified by the distinctly reduced PL spectra intensities (Fig. 5d). It is concluded the loading of CQDs could greatly promote the migration and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and thus more charges could participate in the photocatalytic reactions, facilitating the H2 evolution performance.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized catalysts was systematically evaluated by testing (λ ≥ 400 nm) the H2 escape activity in the photocatalytic process. As depicted in Fig S4, the H2 release rates of the serial photocatalysts were all unceasingly accelerated with the extension of irradiation time. After 4 h reaction, the total H2 yield of pure CTF-1 was 2.76 μmol, which was boosted to 8.17 μmol when 0.24% CQDs was introduced. The photocatalytic H2 production performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts followed the order as: C0.24/CTF > C0.4/CTF > C0.8/CTF > C0.08/CTF > CTF-1 (Fig. 7 a). The optimal H2 evolution activity of 102 μmol∙g−1∙h−1 was achieved in C0.24/CTF, exhibiting three-fold improvement to the pristine CTF-1 (34.5 μmol∙g−1∙h−1). Comparably, the AQY of C0.24/CTF and pristine CTF-1 was calculated to be 0.73 and 0.25% at 400 nm, respectively. It is noteworthy that excessive CQDs loading was not propitious to H2 evolution, since excitation and transmission of photoinduced electrons were probably inhibited by the reduced visible-light utilization due to the aggregation and light scattering of massive CQDs [56]. The photocatalytic stability of C0.24/CTF was then investigated by running five continuous test cycles with each run of 4 h. No remarkable deactivation was detected after five cyclic tests, highlighting the exceptional stability of C0.24/CTF (Fig. 7b) [57]. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of fresh and used C0.24/CTF were compared and no noticeable variation was observed, further confirming the delightful stability of the C0.24/CTF catalyst (Fig. S5). Eventually, the wavelength control experiment was conducted to evaluate the effect of light absorption capacity on the photocatalytic H2 production activity of C0.24/CTF. As speculated, the H2 yield rate reduced with the increase of incident light wavelength and the highest value of 491 μmol∙g−1∙h−1 appeared under 380 nm, showing consistency with the light absorbance spectrum of C0.24/CTF (Fig. 7c).

Based upon the above results of the improved visible-light harvesting, suitable band structure and the accelerated separation/transfer rate of the photoinduced charges, the probable mechanism for elevated photocatalytic H2 evolution were proposed (Fig. 8 ). Upon the visible-light irradiation, CTF-1 were excited and the photoinduced electrons were prompted from the VB to the CB of CTF-1. In this stage, the modified CQDs could serve as the “photosensitizer” to broaden the visible-light responsive range of CTF-1 through its up-conversion fluorescence effect. The unique property of CQDs was attributed to the multiphoton active process, where CQDs simultaneously captured and absorbed two or more photons to emit shorter-wavelength visible lights due to the surface emission traps and the quantum size effect of CQDs [58,59].

Specifically, the wavelength of the CQDs emitted lights kept almost fixed at different excited wavelengths. The excitation-independent behavior of CQDs demonstrated that the emission occurred from the lowest single state irrespective of the excitation mode [60,61]. It was inferred that the specific short-wavelength lights emitted by CQDs could be utilized to excite CTF-1 to produce more photoinduced electrons. To verify the speculation, the photocurrent responses of CTF-1 and CQDs modified catalysts were compared under specific long-wavelength visible-light irradiation (λ = 500 nm). The photocurrent density of CTF-1 was nearly negligible under 500 nm since the energy of incident light (corresponding to 2.48 eV) couldn't activate the pristine CTF-1 (band gap = 2.94 eV). In contrast, the CQDs modified CTF-1 exhibited strong photocurrent density (Fig. 7d), strongly verifying the modification of CQDs was in favor of facilitating visible-light absorption and the solar energy utilization of CTF-1. After the light irradiation stage, the CB electrons were transferred to the surface of the decorated CQDs for water splitting to produce H2, and accordingly the VB holes were quenched by the sacrifice reagent TEOA. Thanks to the excellent electroconductibility, CQDs could serve as the electron library to efficiently inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and significantly accelerate the charge transfer rate in the system [62,63]. Furthermore, in the photocatalytic reaction process, the introduction of CQDs could increase the reaction active sites. The promoted interaction between reactants and catalysts and the activation of reactants also played a pivotal role on the enhancement of photocatalytic performance [26].

A series of CQDs modified CTF-1 semiconductor photocatalysts were finely designed and constructed through a facile impregnation strategy in current work and remarkable enhancement in photocatalytic H2 evolution activity was achieved through optimal CQDs modification. The unique up-conversion fluorescence characteristic of CQDs evidently enlarged the light responsive range and improved the visible-light harvesting of CTF-1. Noteworthily, the lights emitted by CQDs through the multiple-photon active process could be efficiently captured and utilized by CTF-1 to stimulate the photoinduced electrons, which was specifically verified by the photocurrent experiments. Moreover, the decorated CQDs could provide more reaction active sites for adsorption and activation of the small molecular reactant H2O and facilitated the separation and transfer of charge carriers due to its superior electroconductibility. As a totally metal-free, visible-light-driven and environmental-friendly photocatalyst, the CQDs/CTF-1 catalysts showcase considerable superiority and practical viability to be utilized in the field of solar energy conversion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51672047), Youth Talent Support Program of Fujian Province (00387077) and the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01648).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2021.12.220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reaction systems for solar hydrogen production via water splitting with particulate semiconductor photocatalysts

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-propelled jet carbon micromotor enhanced photocatalytic performance for water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dong TG, Sun ML, Hu K, Wang Q, Lu CH, Kou JH. Self-propelled jet carbon micromotor enhanced photocatalytic performance for water splitting. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2021;46:17187-17196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts for artificial photosynthesis and environmental remediation: are we a step closer to achieving sustainability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ong WJ, Tan LL, Ng YH, Yong ST, Chai SP. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts for artificial photosynthesis and environmental remediation: are we a step closer to achieving sustainability. Chem Rev 2016;116:7159-7329.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in anion doped g-C3N4 photocatalysts: a review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple cation exchange approach to Bi-doped ZnS hollow spheres with enhanced UV and visible-light photocatalytic H2-production activity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts: synthesis, properties and its applications in photocatalytic hydrogen generation, CO2 reduction and photodegradation of organic dyes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Reddy CV, Reddy KR, Harish VVN, Shim J, Shankar MV, Shetti NP, Aminabhavi TM.
